narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—higher than the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, family income level, and plans to attend full-time. Among the factors considered, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades stood out as the most influential, pushing the model strongly toward predicting bar failure because it signals performance below what typically leads to success. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA had an equally powerful but opposite effect, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the average of 3.24 seen in students who passed the bar. Your LSAT score played a similar balancing role, nearly matching the 37.27 average for successful bar takers and thus nudging the outlook more positively. While your high family income aligns with a smaller but successful subset of passers, and your full-time status matches the norm for those who succeed, these didn't outweigh the Year 3 decile concern. Compared to typical admitted students—who often rank in higher deciles by Year 3 and show slightly stronger GPAs overall—your profile fell short in that key area, tipping the scales. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid LSAT and full-time commitment showing real potential. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic decile through targeted study habits or additional coursework that demonstrates upward momentum. Retaking the LSAT or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to lift your GPA could also help realign your profile with successful peers. Many students in similar spots have turned things around by addressing these gaps—consider reaching out to advisors for personalized strategies. With persistence, you can reshape this prediction in your favor.
